\tableofcontents
\section{Introduction}

% Introduction to actor models and their communication mechanism.
Actor models provide a computational framework for concurrent systems. In these
systems, the main construct is the notion of an \emph{actor}. Actors are
independent computational units that encapsulate the execution environment of
their processing logic. Asynchronous communication between actors occurs
solely via messages. A message is defined as any piece of data sent from one
actor to another. The reception of these messages is handled by the recipient
actor's \emph{mailbox}, a dedicated piece of storage that preserves message
order while remaining separate from the actor's core logic.

% Challenges in actor system message flow management
One challenge in actor systems is managing message flow under high volumes.
Although some techniques such as rate limiting and \emph{backpressure} help regulate
message rates, these control mechanisms may also fail under heavily loaded
systems. Message delivery is often assumed to be reliable, but practical
implementations really need to consider the possibility of message loss and
delays from processing bottlenecks or crashing actors.

% Proposes a solution by decoupling mailboxes from processing actors.
To address some of these challenges, in particular the need to handle message
loss and delays in processing messages, we propose decoupling mailboxes
from \emph{processing actors}, which are the actors that carry out the processing
logic. In this design, communication occurs through message passing to
dedicated \emph{mailbox actors}, with processing actors pulling messages only
when ready to handle them. This approach is simple, allowing us to give an explicit
specification of the message-passing mechanism, where the mailbox actors remain
opaque to both senders and receivers to maintain compatibility with existing actor
frameworks.

% Outlines the benefits of the proposed architectural separation.
The architectural separation of mailboxes from processing actors provides
several key benefits. First, it cleanly separates message management from
processing logic, allowing mailbox actors to specialize in queue management
while processing actors focus on their core functionality. Second, it enables
flexible message scheduling—different mailbox implementations can support
priority queues, batch processing, or custom dispatch strategies without affecting
processing actors. Third, mailbox actors become modular components that can be
modified or replaced independently.

% Discusses the trade-offs and potential drawbacks of the design.
This design may come with some trade-offs. The primary drawback we foresee is
increased system complexity due to additional actors and message-passing
overhead. This manifests as higher resource usage from running more actors and
potential latency increases when mailbox actors run on different nodes than
their processing actors. However, with careful design, these costs can be
outweighed by the benefits of a more flexible and scalable system.

% Mentions the potential for future work in extending the design.
The design could be extended to support multiple mailbox actors per processing
actor, with minimal additional complexity due to the mailbox actors' simplicity
and well-defined message interface. However, we leave this extension as future
work since it is not required to convey the core design.

% How this paper is organized and what is the contribution.

\paragraph{Contributions}
First, we propose a new architectural
separation between processing actors and their mailboxes in \Cref{sec:model},
which enables improved flexibility and scalability for message management. Second,
we formulate operational semantics in \Cref{sec:semantics} for both the decoupled
architecture and its explicit message-passing mechanism, making the system's
communication patterns formally explicit. This description provides sufficient
grounding to implement a library on top of a compatible system/platform such
as Erlang, and to formalize and prove key system properties such as liveness
guarantees and correctness under concurrent message processing.

\section{Notation}\label{sec:notation}

We use an informal type-theoretic notation. For example, a term $x$ of type $A$
is denoted as $x : A$. Capital letters or capitalized words are used for types,
and lowercase letters or lowercase words are used for terms. There is a universe
of types $U$, so if $A$ is a type, then $A : U$. We also work with inductive
types presented in the following form:

\begin{align*}
  A ::= \cdots \mid \mathsf{C}(A_1, \dots, A_n) \, ,
\end{align*}

where $\mathsf{C}$ is a constructor of the type $A$, and $A_1, \dots, A_n$ are
the types of the constructor arguments. The inductive type of natural numbers is
denoted as $\N$. The terms of this type are denoted by number as usual. Another
recurrent type is the type of parametric lists, denoted as $\mathsf{List}(A)$.
The definition that term $x$ \emph{is} $y$ is denoted as $x :\equiv y$.
Definitionally equal terms are denoted as $a \equiv b$.

Some types are equipped with structure that behave as sets, and here are common
set-theoretic notation we use: the membership relation is denoted as $\in$, so
$a \in A$ is a proposition that $a$ is an element of $A$ (do not confuse with
typing $a : A$). For $A$ and $B$, $A \subseteq B$ denotes set inclusion, meaning
every element of $A$ belongs to $B$ and $\varnothing$ denotes the empty set.
Union between sets $A$ and $B$ is denoted as $A \cup B$. The intersection is
denoted as $A \cap B$. The difference between $A$ and $B$ is denoted as $A
\setminus B$; and $A \times B$ denotes the Cartesian product of $A$ and $B$, and
their terms are denoted as $\langle a,b \rangle$, where $a : A$ and $b : B$. A
\emph{tuple} $\langle a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n \rangle$ is a generalisation of an
ordered pair to an ordered $n$-tuple, where $n \in \N$, of type $A_1 \times
\cdots \times A_n$, and $a_i$ is of type $A_i$ for all $i \in \{1, \dots, n\}$.
If the tuple components are named using sans-serif letters, these names are used
as projections, otherwise, the projections are $\pi_i(a) :\equiv a_i$ for all $i
\in \{1, \dots, n\}$. For example, if $x :\equiv \langle \mathsf{fst},
\mathsf{snd} \rangle$ denotes a tuple with components values can be projected
using $\mathsf{fst}$ and $\mathsf{snd}$ via $\mathsf{fst}(x)$ and
$\mathsf{snd}(x)$, respectively. Additionally, $A_1 \oplus \cdots \oplus A_n$
denotes the disjoint union of $A_1, \dots, A_n$, so $\mathsf{inj_i}(a_i)$ are
the corresponding injections into $A_1 \oplus \cdots \oplus A_n$ from $A_i$ for
all $i \in \{1, \dots, n\}$. Given a type $A$, we can define the optional type
$\Option{A}$ as $A \oplus \bot$, where $\bot$ is the bottom type. And the terms
$\mathsf{none}$ and $\mathsf{some}(a)$ are the corresponding terms for the
optional type when $a : A$.

\section{Model}\label{sec:model}

This section presents a version based on the Engine Model \cite{engine-art}
without any time component. In this actor-like model, the system consists of
\emph{engines}, which are similar to the notion of actor in Agha's Actor Model
\cite{actor-model} but are equipped with more context.

An engine comprises three main components: a \emph{configuration}, an
\emph{execution environment}, and a \emph{behaviour}. The configuration
includes, among other things, the engine's name, location within the system, and
an optional reference to the parent engine. The execution environment includes
an \emph{address book} of other engines to communicate with, including the
engine's own address. Finally, the behaviour defines how the engine reacts to
messages that follow the engine's \emph{message interface}, which is simply a
set of message types that the engine can process. The engine's behaviour is a
set of \emph{guarded actions} consisting of pairs of pre-conditions and actions
to execute. When an engine receives a message, it checks which of its guarded
actions have satisfied pre-conditions, and executes the corresponding action.

\begin{remark}
  The way engine behaviour is defined is similar to the Event-B formalism in
  that event-driven system transitions are specified by a set of pre-conditions
  on sets that form the current system state and the actions to execute if those
  pre-conditions are satisfied. The actions are defined using a language derived
  from process calculi, which often include operations such as parallel composition,
  a null operation, sequencing, nondeterministic choice, and recursion. 
  %Here, we have a more restricted set of actions, as will be seen in \Cref{def:effects}.
\end{remark}

\subsection{Definitions}\label{sec:definitions}

The model assumes an ambient \emph{system} $\kappa$ mainly consisting of a set of
\emph{nodes} $\mathcal{N}$, a set of \emph{engines} $\mathcal{E}$, following a
specific \emph{message-passing mechanism} (MPM for short) that will be described
later, among other components.

Engines are distributed across the nodes of the system, with each engine being
associated with a unique node. Each entity in the system has a unique identifier,
allowing nodes and engines to be denoted as $n_i$ and $e_j$ respectively, where
$i, j \in \N$.

Following the message-passing paradigm, the MPM enforces policies for message
delivery and flow control. This mechanism operates on a set of \emph{messages}
that we denote here as $\mu$. Each message contains information about its
sender and receiver. 

To reason about the system, we use a state machine that evolves in discrete
steps. So, there is a notion of \say{previous} and \say{next} for each entity
with respect to the system, including the set of messages $\mu$. The steps are
denoted using superscripts, so $\kappa^i$ is the $i$-th step of the system, and
$\mu^i$ is the set of messages in the $i$-th step.

% Within the system, each entity has a unique identifier. However, engines can also be
% referenced by name rather than by address. Engine names provide a more legible way
% to refer to engines in the system, making them easier to work with. Since names
% are unique and unambiguous, they can be used interchangeably with addresses when
% referring to engines.


\subsubsection{Addresses and names}\label{sec:addresses-and-names}

\begin{definition}\label{def:address}
An \textbf{address} for an engine $e_j$ within the system is a tuple $\langle n_i, \mathsf{id}_j \rangle$, where
$n_i$ is the node identifier where the engine lives, and $\mathsf{id}_j$ is the engine's unique identifier.
\end{definition}

\begin{axiom}\label{ax:address}
Each engine is uniquely identified by an \emph{address} in the system.
\end{axiom}


\subsubsection{Message Structure}\label{sec:message-structure}

\begin{definition}\label{def:messages} Engine communication occurs through typed
messages. A message is a triple $\langle s,\, t,\, v \rangle$ where $s$ is the
sender address, $t$ is the target address, and $v$ is the payload of type
$\mathsf{Msg_j}$ that is specific to the message types the target engine can
process.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}\label{def:message-interface}
  The \textbf{message interface} of an engine $e_j$ is the set of message types
  $\mathsf{Msg_j}$ that the engine can process.
\end{definition}

To refer to an arbitrary message type, we use the type $\mathsf{Msg}$, which is
the union of all valid message types, that is, $\mathsf{Msg} :\equiv
\bigoplus_{j} \mathsf{Msg_j}$ where $j$ ranges over all engines in the system.

\subsubsection{Engine components}\label{sec:engine-components}

An engine is a tuple of components, which may contain immutable data such as their name,
location, and parent. Although, other information such as its status and environment
can be changed.

\begin{definition}\label{def:engine-status}
The \textbf{status} of an engine $e_j$, of type $\mathsf{EngineStatus}$, determines
the engine's operational mode and thus its lifecycle.
\begin{equation}\label{eq:engine-status}
\mathsf{EngineStatus} := \mathsf{ready} \mid \mathsf{busy} \mid \mathsf{terminated}  \, .
\end{equation}
% tikz
\begin{figure}[!hbp]
\centering
\[\begin{tikzcd}
	& {\mathsf{ready}} \\
	{\mathsf{terminated}} && {\mathsf{busy}}
	\arrow[dashed, no head, from=1-2, to=2-1]
	\arrow[from=1-2, to=2-3]
	\arrow[shift left=3, from=2-3, to=1-2]
	\arrow[from=2-3, to=2-1]
\end{tikzcd}\]
\caption{An engine operates may assume different stages. Every engine starts in a
\textsf{ready} state waiting for a message, and once it reacts to a message it
changes to \textsf{busy}. Then, depending on its logic, the engine may either
return to \textsf{ready} or \textsf{terminated}.}
\label{fig:engine-status}
\end{figure}

\end{definition}

As illustrated in \Cref{fig:engine-status}, an engine is said to be
\textsf{ready} if it is waiting for a message to process. Being \textsf{busy}
means the engine is currently processing a message. And \textsf{terminated}
means the engine will not process any more messages. In such a case, the system
will remove the engine in the next \emph{step}, and disregard any messages sent
to it.

To operate, an engine needs a \emph{configuration} and an \emph{environment}.
In the configuration, we find what it cannot be changed, such as the engine's name,
location, parent, and \emph{the operational mode}. So, let's define first what 
the operational mode is about.

\begin{definition}\label{def:engine-mode}
The \textbf{operational mode} of an engine $e_j$, of type $\mathsf{EngineMode}$,
determines the engine's operational mode, a tag used by the system to
distinguish the purpose of the engine.
\begin{equation}\label{eq:engine-mode}
\mathsf{EngineMode} := \mathsf{processing} \mid \mathsf{mailbox} \, .
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

As we will see later, the operational mode of an engine determines how the system
routes messages to appropriate engines. Those configured as mailboxes receive
special treatment within the message-passing mechanism. Since the operational mode
becomes immutable once an engine is created, this critical attribute goes into the
engine's configuration.

\begin{definition}\label{def:engine-configuration} The \textbf{configuration} of
an engine $e_j$ often denoted by $\mathsf{cfg}_j$ is a tuple $\langle
\mathsf{name}, \mathsf{location}, \mathsf{parent} , \mathsf{mode} \rangle$ of type $\mathsf{Cfg}$, where $\mathsf{name}$
is the engine's name, $\mathsf{location}$ is the engine's location, $\mathsf{parent}$
is the engine's parent, and $\mathsf{mode}$ is the engine's operational mode as
in \Cref{def:engine-mode}.
\end{definition}

Recall that engines are isolated from each other and process messages
independently. However, an engine's configuration alone is insufficient for
proper operation since some form of state is required. Therefore, each engine
encapsulates its own state as part of its execution context, which we refer to as the
engine's \emph{environment}.

\begin{definition}\label{def:engine-environment}
Given a type $S_j$ representing the local state of an engine $e_j$, an
execution \textbf{environment} of $e_j$ is a tuple $\langle s_j, l_j \rangle$
of type $\mathsf{Env}_j(S_j)$, where $S_j$ is type $S_j$ for the engine's
local state $s_j$, and $l_j$ is the engine's address book, a non-empty list
containing the names of other engines, including its own. $l_j$ is of type
$\mathsf{List}(\mathsf{Addr})$.
\end{definition}

\subsubsection{Execution directives}\label{sec:execution-directives}

The behaviour of an engine is a declarative description of what it does when it
reacts to a message. This description of computation is via an effect system
consisting of commands to the system to be executed at runtime.

\begin{definition}\label{def:effects}
Given a type $S_j$ representing the local state of an engine $e_j$, an
\textbf{effect} is of type $\mathsf{Effect}(S_j)$, which considers all the
possible ways an engine can influence the system by reacting to a message.

\begin{equation}\label{eq:effects}
\begin{aligned} %TODO: review this grammar
\mathsf{Effect}(S_j) := &\quad \mathsf{send}(\mathsf{Addr}, \mathsf{Msg}) & &\text{(message dispatch)} \\
\mid & \quad \mathsf{update}(\mathsf{Env}(S_j)) & &\text{(engine state modification)} \\
\mid & \quad \mathsf{spawn}(\mathsf{Cfg}_j, \mathsf{Env}(S_j)) & &\text{(engine creation)} \\
\mid & \quad \mathsf{chain}(\mathsf{List}(\mathsf{Effect}(S_j))) & &\text{(action sequencing)} \\
\mid & \quad \mathsf{terminate} & &\text{(engine shutdown)} \\
\mid & \quad \mathsf{nop} & &\text{(null operation)\, .}
\end{aligned}
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

% TODO: mention how this induces a monad.
% also mention CCS?
\begin{figure}[!htbp]
% https://q.uiver.app/#
\[\begin{tikzcd}
	{} & {(m, \mathsf{cfg_j},\mathsf{env}_j)} & {} \\
	\\
	{r_1} & \cdots & {r_k} \\
	{e_1:\mathsf{Effect}(S_j)} & \cdots & {e_k:\mathsf{Effect}(S_j)}
	\arrow[curve={height=24pt}, shorten <=24pt, shorten >=24pt, dashed, no head, from=1-1, to=1-3]
	\arrow["{g_1}"', maps to, from=1-2, to=3-1]
	\arrow["{g_i}"', maps to, from=1-2, to=3-2]
	\arrow["{g_k}", maps to, from=1-2, to=3-3]
	\arrow["{c_1}"', maps to, from=3-1, to=4-1]
	\arrow[maps to, from=3-2, to=4-2]
	\arrow["{c_k}", maps to, from=3-3, to=4-3]
\end{tikzcd}\]
\caption{For an engine $e_j$, its behaviour acts on a message $m$ and an engine's
configuration and environment $\mathsf{cfg}_j$ and $\mathsf{env}_j$,
respectively. The pre-conditions, denoted as $g_i$, are guards that determine
which action is executed. The symbols $r_i$ represent output that feeds into the
actions $c_i$. The actions, denoted as $c_i$, are effects that are executed if their
pre-conditions are satisfied. If no pre-conditions are satisfied, the action is a
null operation, $\mathsf{nop}$.}
\label{fig:effect}
\end{figure}

Given an engine $e_j$, the following definition specifies the guard and action
components of an engine's behaviour.

\begin{definition}\label{def:guards}
A \textbf{guard} is a pre-condition that, when an engine processes a message
with its environment $\mathsf{env}_j$ and configuration $\mathsf{cfg}_j$,
returns $\mathsf{none}$ if the guard is not satisfied, and returns data otherwise.
Therefore, a guard can be seen as a mapping of type
$\mathsf{Msg} \times \mathsf{Cfg}_j \times \mathsf{Env}_j(S_j) \to \Option{R}$
for some type $R$, where $R$ is the specific type of data returned when the guard is satisfied.
\end{definition}

On the computational aspects of an engine, the behaviour evaluating the different
guards, and for each guard, the action to be executed, whenever the guard is
satisfied, is as follows:

\begin{definition}\label{def:action}
Given an engine $e_j$, an \textbf{action} acting on data of type $R$ is a mapping of type
$R \times \mathsf{Msg}_j \times \mathsf{Cfg}_j \times \mathsf{Env}_j(S_j) \to \mathsf{Effect}(S_j)$.
\end{definition}

Then, we compose a guard and an action to form a \emph{guarded action}, which is
the building block of which an engine's behaviour consists of. The separation of
guard and action allows for a more modular design. Consider that one may want to
reuse the guard of an engine in different engines or decouple the logic of the
guard against the action, allowing the pre-conditions to be reused in scenarios
where the action is not applicable.

\begin{definition}\label{def:guarded-action}
  Given an engine $e_j$, a \textbf{guarded action} of the engine's behaviour is a tuple $\langle g_j, a_j \rangle$, where:
  \begin{itemize}
    \item $g_j$ is a guard as in \Cref{def:guards} that determines when the
    action $a_j$ should execute, and
    \item $a_j$ is an action of type $\mathsf{Effect}(S_j)$ as in \Cref{def:effects} that specifies what to execute
  \end{itemize}
\end{definition}

Together, both the guard and action induce a mapping of type $\mathsf{Msg} \times \mathsf{Cfg}_j
\times \mathsf{Env}_j(S_j) \to \mathsf{Effect}(S_j)$. This mapping takes in a
message, engine configuration, and environment, and outputs the effect to
execute when the guard is satisfied.

So, upon receiving a message, an engine $e_j$ checks which of its guards is
satisfied, and if so, the engine executes the corresponding action, as
illustrated in \Cref{fig:effect}.

\begin{remark}\label{rem:turing-completeness}
A question at this point is whether engines can enable unbounded computation.
Engines' behaviours as introduced in~\cite{engine-art} are Turing complete. To see
this, we need to show that the design satisfies the two fundamental requirements:
conditional branching through guard-state interactions and indefinite looping
via message-passing recursion. Note that the message-passing mechanism
says nothing about forbidding engines from sending messages to themselves, and
this is a powerful mechanism that allows for recursive patterns. So, while
individual effects as listed in \Cref{def:effects} lack explicit looping constructs,
and it may not seem at first glance there is a recursive mechanism, the combination
of guarded actions acting as conditional statements with open-ended message passing
enables computational universality.
\end{remark}

\subsubsection{Engines}\label{sec:processing-engines}

Having defined the components that make up an engine's behaviour, we can now
formally specify engines themselves. As the fundamental computational units of
our system, engines encapsulate both the processing logic and state management
capabilities. We have established the types of their constituent parts - guards,
actions, configurations and environments. Now we can provide a complete
specification of the engine type.

\begin{definition}\label{def:processing-engine}   
An \textbf{engine} is the computational unit of the system, formally specified by a tuple
$\langle b, r, \mathsf{cfg}, \mathsf{env} \rangle$ where:
\begin{itemize}
\item $b$ is the engine's behaviour --a set of guarded actions as defined in \Cref{def:guarded-action},
\item $r$ is the engine's status flag as specified in \Cref{def:engine-status},
\item $\mathsf{cfg}$ is the engine's configuration as described in
\Cref{def:engine-configuration}, and
\item $\mathsf{env}$ is the engine's execution environment as outlined in \Cref{def:engine-environment}.
\end{itemize}
\end{definition}

% \begin{remark}\label{rem:engine-init}
% No need to initialize an engine. Once declared, an engine is ready to process
% messages.
% \end{remark}

Two flavours of engines exist in our system: processing engines and mailbox
engines. A processing engine is any engine marked as
$\mathsf{processing}$ in its configuration via \textsf{mode}. Otherwise, the
engine is a mailbox engine, however, we require a bit more.

\begin{definition}\label{def:mailbox-engine} A \emph{mailbox engine} $m_j$
paired with a processing engine $e_j$ is an engine that satisfies the following requirements:
\begin{itemize}
\item the engine's operational mode is $\mathsf{mailbox}$,
\item the engine's parent is the processing engine $e_j$,
\item the engine's message interface is fixed and consists of only two message types: 
$\mathsf{append}$ and $\mathsf{pull}$ both of which take a single message of
type $\mathsf{Msg_j}$ as the payload the engine reads from and writes to,
\item the state of the mailbox engine must implement a data structure that
supports the two message types,
\item the engine's behaviour must ensure that the $\mathsf{append}$ message
is always executed before the $\mathsf{pull}$ message, and
\item the engine's behaviour must ensure that the $\mathsf{pull}$ message is
always executed after the $\mathsf{append}$ message.
\end{itemize}
\end{definition}

With mailbox engines added to the system, we have a strict producer-consumer
pattern introduced through explicit pull semantics, decoupling message storage
from processing logic. This allows for independent scaling of message ingestion
and core computation.

\subsubsection{Nodes}\label{sec:system-state}

Before we little mention the system, $\kappa$,  mainly consisting of a set of
nodes $\mathcal{N}$, a set of engines $\mathcal{E}$, following a specific
message-passing mechanism, still unknown. To address this, we give explicit
definitions to missing components, which are the nodes, and the global
message-passing mechanism.

\begin{definition}\label{def:node} A \textbf{node} in $\mathcal{N}$ is a tuple
$\langle \mathsf{id}_i, \mathcal{E}_i, \omega_i \rangle$ where $\mathsf{id}_i$ is the
node's unique identifier, $\mathcal{E}_i$ is the set of engines living on the
node, and $\omega_i$ representing specific node's capabilities and non-derivable
operations.
\end{definition}

% What could be the capabilities and non-derivable operations of a node?
% 1. Distribute the system across multiple machines.
% 2. Provide a specific message-passing mechanism.
% 3. Provide a specific engine factory.
% 4. Provide a specific engine configuration like putting information in the
%    engine's configuration.
% 5. Specific services like authentication, authorization, and accounting.
% I'm guessing certain nodes would provide specific special-purpose services.


\begin{remark}\label{rem:node-functions}
Along with any node $n_i$, we derive the following functions:
\begin{itemize}
\item $\alpha_i : \mathcal{E}_i \to \mathsf{Addr}$ as the mapping that projects out the addresses of the engines
living on node $n_i$, and
\item $\mathsf{mailboxOf} : \mathcal{E} \to \mathsf{Addr}$ as the mapping that
projects out the address of the dedicated mailbox engine for a given processing
engine instance.
\end{itemize}
\end{remark}

As mentioned, the model presented here is a variation of the Engine Model
\cite{engine-art}, where the authors define the formal model \textsc{DETCs} which
extends communication-based system models with a time component and dynamic
process (engine) creation. While we retain the latter capability, we leave the
temporal aspect for future work. \textsc{DETCs} employ a labelled transition
system semantics and here we do too. Transitions in the state machine occur when
a process/engine reacts to a message by updating its state, generating new
messages and engines, or simply by doing nothing.

To picture how the system evolves, as any state machine, we must define the state.
The transition rules are then defined as the rules that govern how these states
evolve, and we address this next.

\begin{definition}\label{def:global-config} A \textbf{state} of the system
$\kappa$ is a tuple $\kappa^{i} \equiv \langle \mathcal{N}^i, \mu^i, \omega^i
\rangle$ where,
\begin{itemize}
\item $\mathcal{N}^i$ is the set of nodes,
\item $\mu^i$ is the set of in-transit messages representing ongoing communications, and
\item $\omega^i$ represents the system capabilities and operations.
\end{itemize}
\end{definition}

The superscript $i$ in $\kappa^i$ denotes an instantiation of the system. This
can be viewed as a snapshot of the system at a point in time denoted by that
superscript, with the ability to evolve to a successor state $\kappa^{i+1}$.

The associated state machine in a \textsc{DETC} is defined in terms of active
processes and a set of messages $M$ awaiting delivery, assuming all get
delivered. This is an assumption we also adopt here.

\begin{axiom}\label{ax:message-delivery}
For any engines $e, e' \in \mathcal{E}$ and message $m \in M$ in transit within
system state $\kappa$, there exists a subsequent state $\kappa'$ where $m$ is
eventually delivered to its intended recipient.
\end{axiom}

Transitioning from one state to a next state gives rise to a transition rule,
and the opportunity to define new system capabilities and operations, add new,
remove existing nodes, engines, and the included services provided by the
system. To all this can happen, the system at any point in time provide
functions such as the following.

\begin{itemize}
\item $\mathsf{freshId} : \mathcal{N} \to \mathsf{Id}$ is the function that
generates a fresh identifier for node creation.
\item $\mathsf{freshName} : \mathcal{N} \to \mathsf{Name}$ is the function that
generates a fresh name for an engine.
\item $\execEffect$ is the function that executes effects as in \Cref{def:effects}
and outputs the new system state after the effect has been executed.
\end{itemize}

Also, we extend the functions defined in \Cref{rem:node-functions}. That is,
the node-specific functions $\alpha_i$ and $\mathsf{mailboxOf}_i$ can be used to
construct global versions $\alpha$ and $\mathsf{mailboxOf}$ that operate over
all engines $\mathcal{E}$ in $\kappa^i$, functions of type $\mathcal{E} \to
\mathsf{Addr}$. These global functions are defined by combining the node-specific
functions across all nodes, allowing us to look up addresses for any engine in
look up addresses for any engine in the system regardless of which node it resides
on.

There must be other functions that allow to add, remove, and modify nodes,
engines, and the included services provided by the system. We leave the
specification of these functions for future work.

\section{Operational Semantics}\label{sec:semantics}

We formalise the reduction rules for our operational semantics through the
relation $\kappa_n \to \kappa_{n+1}$, where $\kappa_n \equiv (\alpha_n, \mu_n)$
represents the system state. Mailbox engines are modeled as sets for notational
convenience, with message operations handled through message passing.

\subsection{System Transition Rules}

\begin{definition}[\textsf{Send}]\label{rule:send}
Processing engine $e$ sends message $v$ to target $e_{\mathsf{target}}$:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:send}
\frac{
  \alpha(e) \equiv \langle b, m_e, r, \mathsf{cfg}, \mathsf{env} \rangle \quad
  m_{\mathsf{target}} \equiv \mathsf{mailboxOf}(e_{\mathsf{target}}) \quad
  v : \mathsf{Msg}
}{
  (\alpha, \mu) \to (\alpha, \mu \cup \{ \langle e, m_{\mathsf{target}}, v \rangle \})
}
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\textsf{Append}]\label{rule:append}
Mailbox engine $e$ enqueues message $v$:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:append}
\frac{
  \alpha(e) \equiv q \quad
  \langle s, e, v \rangle \in \mu
}{
  (\alpha, \mu) \to (\alpha[e \mapsto q \cup \{v\}], \mu \setminus \{\langle s, e, v \rangle\})
}
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\textsf{Pull}]\label{rule:pull}
Processing engine retrieves message from mailbox:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:pull}
\frac{
  \alpha(e) \equiv \langle b, m_e, \mathsf{ready}, \mathsf{cfg}, \mathsf{env} \rangle \quad
  \alpha(m_e) \equiv \{v\} \cup q
}{
  (\alpha, \mu) \to (\alpha', \mu)
}
\end{equation}
where $\alpha' \equiv \alpha[e \mapsto \langle b, m_e, \mathsf{busy}, \mathsf{cfg}, \mathsf{env} \rangle] \cup \{ m_e \mapsto q \}$.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\textsf{Clean}]\label{rule:clean}
Remove terminated engine:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:clean}
\frac{
  \alpha(e) \equiv \langle b, m_e, \mathsf{terminated}, \mathsf{cfg}, \mathsf{env} \rangle
}{
  (\alpha, \mu) \to (\alpha \setminus \{e\}, \mu)
}
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\textsf{Process}]\label{rule:process}
Engine processes message:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:process}
\frac{
  \alpha(e) \equiv \langle b, m_e, \mathsf{busy}, \mathsf{cfg}, \mathsf{env} \rangle \quad
  b, \mathsf{env} \vdash v \Downarrow E \quad
  \execEffect(E, e, (\alpha, \mu)) \equiv (\alpha', \mu', r')
}{
  (\alpha, \mu) \to (\alpha', \mu')
}
\end{equation}
where $r' \equiv \mathsf{ready}$ if $r' \neq \mathsf{terminated}$.
\end{definition}

\subsection{Effect Execution Rules}

The function $\execEffect(E, a, (\alpha, \mu))$ is defined recursively:

\begin{definition}[\textsf{E-sendMsg}]\label{rule:e-send}
\begin{equation}\label{eq:e-send}
\frac{
  \mathsf{msg} \equiv \langle e_{\mathsf{sender}}, e_{\mathsf{target}}, v \rangle \quad
  m_{\mathsf{target}} \equiv \mathsf{mailboxOf}(e_{\mathsf{target}})
}{
  \execEffect(\mathsf{sendMsg}(\mathsf{msg}), a, (\alpha, \mu)) \equiv ((\alpha, \mu \cup \{ \mathsf{msg} \}), \mathsf{ready})
}
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\textsf{E-updateEnv}]\label{rule:e-update}
\begin{equation}\label{eq:e-update}
\frac{
  \alpha(e) \equiv \langle b, m_e, r, \mathsf{cfg}, \mathsf{env} \rangle \quad
  \mathsf{env}^{*} : \mathsf{ofType}(\mathsf{env})
}{
  \execEffect(\mathsf{updateEnv}(\mathsf{env}^{*}), e, (\alpha, \mu)) \equiv (\alpha[e \mapsto \langle b, m_e, r, \mathsf{cfg}, \mathsf{env}^{*} \rangle], \mathsf{ready})
}
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\textsf{E-spawnEngine}]\label{rule:e-spawn}
Create new engine:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:e-spawn}
\frac{
  n \equiv \mathsf{freshId}(\alpha, \mu) \quad
  m \equiv \mathsf{freshId}(\alpha, \mu) \quad
  \alpha(e) \equiv \langle b, m_e, r, \mathsf{cfg}, \mathsf{env} \rangle
}{
  \execEffect(\mathsf{spawnEngine}(e), a, (\alpha, \mu)) \equiv (\alpha \cup \{ n \mapsto \langle b, m, \mathsf{ready}, \mathsf{cfg}, \mathsf{env} \rangle \}, \mathsf{ready})
}
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\textsf{E-chain}]\label{rule:e-chain}
Sequential composition:

\begin{equation}\label{eq:e-chain}
\frac{
  \execEffect(e_1, a, (\alpha, \mu)) \equiv (\alpha_1, \mu_1, s_1) \quad
  \execEffect(\mathsf{chain}(e_2; \dots; e_n), a, (\alpha_1, \mu_1)) \equiv (\alpha', \mu', s')
}{
  \execEffect(\mathsf{chain}(e_1; \dots; e_n), e, (\alpha, \mu)) \equiv (\alpha', \mu', s')
}
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

The complete operational semantics comprises three components:
\begin{enumerate}
\item Configuration transitions via Rules \textsf{Send}--\textsf{Process}
\item Effect execution through Rules \textsf{E-sendMsg}--\textsf{E-chain}
\item Type safety through Definition~\ref{def:effects}
\end{enumerate}

This system formalises message passing (via Rules~\ref{rule:send}--\ref{rule:append}), message processing (Rule~\ref{rule:process}), and effect-driven state changes as defined in Section~\ref{sec:semantics}.

\section{Related Work}\label{sec:related}

Prior work has explored decoupling mailboxes from actors, primarily through
techniques like work-queues where actors pull messages when ready to process
them. Libraries like WeChaty in JavaScript have implemented similar mailbox
separation patterns. However, our work is novel in treating mailboxes as
first-class primitives within the actor model itself.
